item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the subtle implication that the previous users aren't around anymore because their own creations killed them somehow.

The spider especially seems a likely cause.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A black, spider-like entity measuring approximately two meters in height. Each leg ends in an ornate, wide-bladed sword of unique design and coloration. Creature's abdomen is on fire, with no apparent detriment.

Are there irons in that fire? I think there are irons in that fire. How many? Probably a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to admit that the format deviation annoyed me, but that's mainly because newbies get incinerated on the spot when they attempt such a thing and I hate double standards (and because I consider myself a newbie as well).

Basically the reason I downvoted. Plus, it's really just a location from a TV show turned into an SCP. You don't really do anything with that concept other than make it Wondertainment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly don't like this, simply because Fred Rogers would never associate with someone with the blatant disregard for the safety of children that Wondertainment has. I mean, the central theme of a significant majority Wondertainment is "Oh ho, isn't this so much fun kids? Just be careful or…oh, you're already dead/dismembered/dimensionally dislocated, huh?" Even if Wondertainment guaranteed Rogers that the Magical Sandbox World O' Magic™ was 100% safe (and 550 kph locomotives are in no way safe), I cannot believe that Rogers would overlook Wondertainment's other atrocities. I imagine Dr. Wontertainment to be much like Willy Wonka, in that he makes products for children that do marvelous, magical things but has a complete disregard for the well-being of absolutely anyone else besides himself, which ends up getting people hurt (and I don't believe Rogers would have associated with Wonka, either). That is the complete opposite of Fred Rogers' outlook. The man honestly cared about each and every person he met, and associating him with Wondertainment brings the whole article crashing down for me.

Aggressively non-standard format aside, this was hovering on the edge of neutral/upvote. Associating Rogers with a psychopath is heavy downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote ftw! (for the whimsy).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This hits something deep and nostalgic in my heart and I'm a sucker for that. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Too ambitious - too many details covered too shallowly. Doesn't feel well put together, doesn't feel… is "syncretic" the word I'm looking for? Or maybe "consolidated?"

It spreads itself thin. And it's too obviously a skip made from a real TV show. I feel like it could do with half the examples. The conductor is great, but the interview is, as has been said, on-the-nose. I could care more about the threat level fustian; it's not all that important to me, though it does feel like a double standard as was said upthread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand where you're coming from. The Bear Train part caught me off guard as a bit incongruous at first, but looking over the whole thing, I think it makes sense.

But the "Doomspider" and its associates are what make me laugh the most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea a lot, and the execution is good. My only complaints are that the final line of the interview makes it a bit too obvious - and also that I didn't get the reference at the end which annoyed me. I hate seeing references which I know I won't get. Especially when they're cultural ones.

Apart from that - all good! Didn't even notice the threat level. Meant nothing to me either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's slapdash because we have multiple people coming at the 'problem' from different angles. If it was really being thought through, there'd be some uniformity in the approach. (And, I would think, some actual discussion on the site before people just started single-handedly changing things to suit their needs.)

…Actually, Chubert said everything I wanted to. As for the "find other outlets" thing, I hear good stuff about the Library. It just seems like, to me, a few people have gotten bored with the Foundation, and it might behoove them to consider moving on. We'd certainly lose out for such a thing happening, don't get me wrong, as it seems to be a lot of our most well-established and creative writers who have this issue.

Honestly, what needs to happen next is site-wide discussion of the problem. And the main question that needs to be asked is is this necessary for the site?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, shit!

I didn't get that.

Yeah, I bet he does know Dr. Wondertainment.

I also thought you meant Fred the scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the first point, I don't think that's what TL333s is saying. Well, I can't really know what he's saying, but I think I agree with him, and this is what I'm saying: an article is not the place to make cosmetic changes to the medium. In-story, meta changes are obviously fine, but we're not, like, poets; we're not experimenting with the form of a poem. Tales and such are fine.

And obviously he's not saying that people should stop writing for the site. In the case of this article, all of three words change if you stick to article format. Keter and Euclid and Safe have each gathered in-universe implications, whether it be silly stuff like "Keter duty" or the general weirdness associated with Euclid or whatever. "Threat Class: Green" doesn't change the article. The article still stands perfectly fine without it. So people can still write for the site; the object class really doesn't matter, and for a lot of people, especially the new folks who are not privy to behind-doors discussions about object class, it's just confusing. Experimentation with format can be done elsewhere—that's the point, I think.

On the second point, I'll flat out say that they're bad ideas, and I will also say that they seem nothing if not slapdash to me, given that I defy you to find two people who are using the same alternate classification system. If it's so well-thought-out, why has there been no coordination between people who seem to want the exact same thing? What's the endgame for the 3-ish people who've proposed alternate systems? Do we have roughly one third the site use one system from now one, one third a second, and one third the last? That's preposterous. It would destroy consistency between articles. It would confuse the fuck out of your casual reader and put us way up that ivory tower.

I haven't seen these discussions regarding alternate classification system, but if changing the classification system is such a great idea, I'd at least expect the people who want it to happen to agree on some plan of action. If any of these alt systems was good for the site, I'd expect to see more than one person use it—although perhaps the reason why people aren't using it is because staff hasn't made any official statement, I don't know. If we waved a magic wand and added a Threat Class to every single one of the 2000+ articles we've written, then I would be 110% down with using it in the future. Given that staff is currently recruiting teams to go through every single one of those 2000+ articles and check whether or not their images are legitimate, adding a Threat Class seems to be a comparable amount of work. There wouldn't be any author permission involved, but there isn't any author permission involved in the image removal process, either.

And regardless of how well thought out these discussions are, once again, a. majority of site has no idea what you're talking about b. the end result is still pretty slapdash.

Anyway, I feel I should probably talk about the article itself. I really liked the setup leading up to the Dr. Manhattan-type desert sandbox of reality-warping creativity, and then I was disappointed by what was, to me, a pretty trite appeal to the Wonder of Childhood Imagination! I was at the upvote/neutral vote threshhold and the fact that I was jolted out of suspension of disbelief in the first couple lines by a Threat Class sealed the deal for neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
maybe they should consider finding other outlets for their creative drive

So… you're saying that people should just stop writing for the site rather than try to make changes that they like? …Why?

Not that they're bad ideas

instead of making slapdash attempts to change it.

(1) These statements look a bit contradictory to me.

(2) Regardless of what you think of these ideas, they're not slapdash attempts, and I know you know that because I've seen you involved in conversations about it. Not liking a change that's been extensively thought out doesn't make it a slapdash attempt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the site really stagnating without there being threat levels or object sub-classes though? Not that they're bad ideas, it just seems to me like a few people are starting to get bored with this site, and maybe they should consider finding other outlets for their creative drive instead of making slapdash attempts to change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, does Mr. Conductor have a little Mister tag on it somewhere?
And if you value your sanity never, ever, try to remove the Zentai.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whether you agree or not with the format being flexible, or like Djoric's particular approach, you have to admit that this is how change happens. And without change, the site stagnates and doesn't improve.

Ultimately, the mob survives. If Djoric's SCPs survive, and if this style (or freedom of style) catches on, then a year from now it could be the established way of doing things and we could be having the whole argument all over again with the next guy's intended changes.

Personally, I'm loving it. It's creative evolution at its best.

I haven't voted yet, Djoric, as I haven't had a chance for a proper readthrough. But be safe knowing that my vote won't hinge on liking or disliking that you've tried a different thing, but rather on how I feel it's been pulled off.

I respect your bravery to stick your creative neck above the parapet. Good show!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you really even have to ask that question?

(that means yes)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
block of concert

Sounds like a SCP in its own right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, does this mean George Carlin is an SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahh. See, you go changing shit, and it loses any meaning, I totally missed that that was even there.

Also, to reiterate, I'll quote a comment further downthread:

I have to admit that the format deviation annoyed me, but that's mainly because newbies get incinerated on the spot when they attempt such a thing and I hate double standards (and because I consider myself a newbie as well).

This is why I consider non-format-screw formatting changes highly arrogant. Just, y'know, to kick that puppy a little more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aah! That makes much more sense. I thought you meant scp-423 ("Fred"), and was trying to figure out how a text-based SCP could enjoy this sandbox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fred Rogers is dead. Long Live Fred Rogers.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to admit that the format deviation annoyed me, but that's mainly because newbies get incinerated on the spot when they attempt such a thing and I hate double standards (and because I consider myself a newbie as well).

But I simply had to upvote at the tree bearing ice cream filled fruits, which reminded me of The Last Continent. And I have a soft spot for strange yet (mostly) harmless SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …ah, then not the lives-in-a-book Fred. That possibility was making my head spin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I'd be happy to see the entire site adopt this format, but personally I'll always find it weird to see one format for you and one for everyone else.

That being said, I believe this is the first of your articles I've downvoted. I'm not saying the format screw is an automatic downvote, it just means I have to find the scip exceptional in order to upvote and I think others are the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Fred mentioned in the end is Fred Rogers, of Mr. Rogers' Neighborhood.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The entire point isn't to change the whole site.

The ideal point is gaining the freedom to write this shit without everyone screeching about four words and downvoting solely about them regardless of the actual quality of the article.

Or, you know, actually talking about the article, instead of the four words that I changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this based on any particular children's show? Because it feels like it should be aimed at someone in particular, but that someone obviously isn't me.

No vote for now…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I have absolutely no problem with Threat Levels and additional classes, it's beginning to get depressing to watch you go it alone, tell everyone you don't care what they have to say about it and then show quite clearly that you don't care if anyone else is going to join in.

I'd understand format screws if you were really lobbying to change the format for everyone, but instead you seem to have just dug your heels in and said "I'm doing this my way, I don't care what you have to say and I don't care if it's not what I'm supposed to do".

Anyway, I know this has been discussed before at length but I just thought I'd throw my opinion in there. I think it's a shame that you'll willingly jeopardise your articles just for the sake of wanting to do it your own way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From my understanding a "hater" is anyone who dislikes and reacts negatively toward a idea, religion,personal trait, ect, for no good reason whatsoever. I dislike the format because it keeps me from feeling a sense mystery about the SCP and gives away traits of it from the very beginning, which A.Keeps me from enjoying it B.Breaks my headcanon of how the Foundation formats it's SCP documents(yes , I know it's selfish but my headcanon is my own and is what I enjoy most)C.Doesn't seem nessacerry with current classification standards. There, three reasons why I dislike it, unless of course I'm incorrect about what the term "hater" means. To further explain, I didn't take the post as a personal attack on me but as a way that could mislabel my "opinions" toward the matter. The mention of my liberty to criticize works was a attempt to show that I was not "jus hatin cus i caz" but to show that I don't criticize for no good reason whatsoever. If I am incorrect please inform me, I hate being ignorant of such basic things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. If you're taking a post aimed at haters as a personal attack, you're tacitly acknowledging that you're a hater.
  2. *allowed
  3. "tempered" doesn't actually mean anything. I think you're looking for "opinionated".
  4. The post was a direct acknowledgement of the fact that people are going to state their own negative opinions of the piece. Asserting that you are permitted to do so is completely irrelevant.
  5. "I'm not a member of [negatively portrayed group] even though I do the exact things the group is accused of because I'm justified" was never a solid argument. Being a hater can be justified if you're hating on something bad, but by refusing to own up to it, you're undermining your own point.

This feels too polished for its own good. Like it's fine-tuned and streamlined to create maximum whimsy density and tie itself up in a nice bow. It checks off a few well-known memes without committing to doing them ironically or unironically, which robs them of any real significance.

It reads like a sterile, calculated attempt at making something fun and cool without the kind of loose and laid-back writing necessary for that. I'm afraid I have to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a whole lot of half… baked… ideas… here…

…

Shit. You turned the Sandbox wiki into a SCP, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, all the other things were made by kids who had managed to go there, so yeah, they'd stick out like a sore thumb.

As for the rest, I have no reason to believe that Mr. Rogers would suddenly not exist in the scipverse, and with that in mind no reason to believe that he and Dr. Wondertainment would not know each other.

And it does have a proper object class. It's Safe. Containment Class is exactly the same as object class, except more specific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My stance on the whole thing:

"NO! Don't you understand, man!? A threat level. A THREAT LEVEL. The entire system is undermined! Our authority is moot! The addition of this single word is going to spread and take hold and BRING THE FOUNDATION TO ITS FUCKING KNEES!"

"Actually, sir, I don't think anyone else is particularly interested in using-"

"Shut up! Can't you see I'm spiraling here!?"

But yeah. Like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some people consider "following the format, unless they have good reason not to" a fairly important merit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not hating when it spoils the article before you read it. I am not a"hater". Tempered yes, hater no. We are aloud to state our personal opinions as long as we are respectful about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whimsical extrauniversal Wide-Open Sandbox world, but the Foundation hasn't figured out all the controls yet?

+1

(not gonna lie, I didn't even notice the slightly different object classification setup until I went to the discussion page.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, no, no, no.

I like that the sapient part of the entity is really complicated: it's not just a guy, it's a talking puppet on a non-talking guy dressed like he's pretending you can't see him.

The rest starts to get overdone, like you had a bunch of ideas for oddball skips that couldn't stand on their own (Bear Train could, I'm pretty sure) and shoved them all into this article.

And then you turn it into The Neighhorhood of Make-Believe, the SCP. No.

(Also I don't mind adding in extra junk like threat levels at the top, but would it kill you to give this a proper object class?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not that the SCP itself that's bad, but when presented in this format it ruins the need to think about it if it states that it posses no harm to people, and yes I like the old "object class" more than "containment class". To me "object class" doesn't refer to wether or not it's a spatial anomaly or a block of concert but to what is in the cell and its threat of escaping and danger combined into one designation, with its threat of escape being the main concern, danger secondary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assume it means people will downvote this article independent of its merits because of the nonstandard object class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Immensely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We're a writing website. Please make an attempt to use proper grammar. As it is, your post is rambling and utterly incomprehensible up until the last line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes and then that can apply to everyone's own lil "special" SCP. It takes way from the article, because it takes away any sense of unknown. It gives away its threat and mystery from the beginning. It lets the reader know that it posses no threat and makes it less interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though competently written, it's a bit too on-the-nose for me, I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Expanded classification, the detailed extra dimensional portal definition system, a helluva lot of whimsy, Isabel, and what I'm fairly sure is a tribute to Mr. Rogers?

Shut up and take my upvote, Djoric. This hits all my buttons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, dunno how I missed that…

Then I'm going to go with a neutral vote. Good, but even as someone who watched a lot of Mr. Rogers as a kid, the reference does nothing for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It wasn't a sudden dash to whimsy: that was established firmly in the beginning.

Consider the item, the location, and who the note was written to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm fine with the class, and overall I like it, although given the fact that the dude was wearing a zentai, I was definitely expecting it to be way more in the vein (heh) of the Corpse Mine. So I guess that the sudden dash to whimsy was a bit weird. Also, I didn't much care for the Wondertainment connection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know that a lot of people are going to downvote because of the non-standard class. But I'd have to say one thing about this skip:

"You are special just the way you are."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And what, exactly, is this supposed to mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Preemptive "haters gonna hate".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to post this tomorrow, but sometimes you just gotta be a little early.
